summary: The q1 dataset shows uniform activity across all ten problems (q1-q10), each with exactly 100 issues and no recorded proof status, suggesting raw or unprocessed tracking data. Without differentiated metrics, no single problem stands out as having more traction, and the absence of proof-status detail limits assessment of solvability. The immediate priority is to enrich the dataset with discriminating signals before meaningful prioritization is possible.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) report an identical count of 100 issues, indicating possible placeholder, capped, or unparsed data rather than genuine differentiation.
Proof status is unpopulated for every problem, preventing any assessment of how close any problem is to resolution.
No per-problem metadata (issue categories, severity, recency) is available to identify activity hotspots or recurring failure patterns.
The uniform distribution makes it impossible to distinguish high-traction problems from stagnant ones.
highlights: Complete coverage: all ten problems are being actively tracked with issues logged.
Consistent data structure across problems provides a clean baseline for further instrumentation.
The volume of 100 issues per problem indicates rich underlying material once properly categorized.

suggested_todos.title: Investigate why all problems show exactly 100 issues — verify against a data cap or parsing error
Populate proof-status fields per problem to enable solvability assessment
Tag issues by category and severity to surface common patterns and hotspots
Add recency and activity timestamps to identify problems with the most current traction
Re-run the insight report once enriched metadata is available
